tagline: Calculate conduit fill capacity based on wire gauge, count, and conduit size per NEC standards

faq.question: What is the NEC conduit fill limit?
faq.answer: The NEC allows 53% fill for one wire, 31% for two wires, and 40% for three or more wires. These limits prevent overheating and allow easy wire pulling.

faq.question: Why does conduit fill matter?
faq.answer: Overfilled conduit causes heat buildup which degrades wire insulation and can cause fires. It also makes pulling and replacing wires extremely difficult.

formulas: Fill % = (Number of Wires x Wire Area) / Conduit Area x 100
NEC Limits: 1 wire = 53%, 2 wires = 31%, 3+ wires = 40%

tagline: Calculate transformer kVA requirements based on load voltage and amperage

faq.question: How do I size a transformer?
faq.answer: Multiply voltage by amperage (and by 1.732 for three-phase), divide by 1000 to get kVA, then add a 25% safety factor. Choose the next standard size up.

faq.question: What safety factor should I use?
faq.answer: A 125% (1.25x) safety factor is standard for most applications. Use 150% for motor loads or environments with frequent load spikes.

formulas: Single Phase kVA = (Voltage x Amps) / 1000
Three Phase kVA = (Voltage x Amps x 1.732) / 1000

tagline: Calculate electric motor horsepower from torque and RPM or voltage and amperage

faq.question: How is horsepower calculated from torque?
faq.answer: HP = (Torque in ft-lbs x RPM) / 5252. The constant 5252 comes from converting ft-lbs/min to horsepower (33,000 ft-lbs/min = 1 HP, and 33000 / (2 x pi) = 5252).

faq.question: What is typical motor efficiency?
faq.answer: Standard motors are 85-90% efficient. Premium efficiency motors reach 92-96%. Efficiency drops at partial loads — a motor running at 50% load may only be 80% efficient.

formulas: HP = (Torque x RPM) / 5252
Electrical HP = Mechanical HP / Efficiency

tagline: Balance electrical loads across panel phases to prevent overloading and tripped breakers

faq.question: Why is panel load balancing important?
faq.answer: Unbalanced loads cause the neutral conductor to carry excess current, leading to overheating, voltage fluctuations, and potential fire hazards. NEC recommends keeping phases within 10-15% of each other.

faq.question: How much imbalance is acceptable?
faq.answer: Keep phase loads within 10-15% of each other. Greater than 20% imbalance can cause flickering lights, tripped breakers, and premature equipment failure.

formulas: Imbalance % = |Phase A - Phase B| / Average Load x 100
Panel Utilization = Max Phase Load / Panel Rating x 100

tagline: Determine the correct grounding conductor size per NEC Table 250.122

faq.question: Can I use aluminum ground wire?
faq.answer: Yes, but it must be larger than copper. Aluminum is not recommended for wet locations or direct burial without proper protection. Most residential work uses copper grounding conductors.

faq.question: What is the difference between grounding and bonding?
faq.answer: Grounding connects the system to earth for voltage stabilization. Bonding connects metal parts together to ensure a safe fault current path. Both are required by NEC for safety.

formulas: Ground wire size is determined by NEC Table 250.122 based on overcurrent device rating
Aluminum ground wire must be two sizes larger than copper equivalent

tagline: Calculate total electrical panel load and remaining capacity for new circuits

faq.question: What is the 80% rule for electrical panels?
faq.answer: NEC requires that continuous loads not exceed 80% of the breaker or panel rating. A 200A panel should carry no more than 160A of continuous load. This provides a safety margin for inrush currents.

faq.question: When should I upgrade my electrical panel?
faq.answer: Upgrade when your panel consistently runs above 75% capacity, when adding major appliances (EV charger, heat pump), or when your panel is over 25 years old with outdated breakers.

formulas: Panel Capacity (kW) = Panel Amps x Voltage / 1000
Utilization % = Current Load / Panel Rating x 100

tagline: Calculate three-phase power, current, and voltage for industrial and commercial electrical systems

faq.question: What is power factor?
faq.answer: Power factor is the ratio of real power (kW) to apparent power (kVA). A PF of 1.0 means all power does useful work. Motors and inductive loads lower PF to 0.7-0.9. Low PF wastes energy and increases utility bills.

faq.question: What is the difference between line and phase voltage?
faq.answer: Line voltage is measured between any two phases (e.g., 480V). Phase voltage is measured from one phase to neutral (480/1.732 = 277V). Lighting often uses phase voltage; motors use line voltage.

formulas: Apparent Power (kVA) = 1.732 x Line Voltage x Line Current / 1000
Real Power (kW) = kVA x Power Factor

tagline: Size an emergency or standby generator based on your essential electrical loads

faq.question: Why does motor starting load matter for generator sizing?
faq.answer: Electric motors draw 3-6x their running current when starting. If the generator cannot handle this surge, it will stall or trip breakers. Always size for the largest motor starting simultaneously with other loads.

faq.question: What power factor should I use?
faq.answer: Use 0.8 for mixed residential loads. Industrial motor loads may be 0.7-0.85. Resistive loads (heaters, lights) are 1.0. Lower power factor means you need a larger generator.

formulas: Running Load = Essential Loads + Motor Loads
Starting Load = Essential Loads + (Motor Load x Starting Multiplier)
Generator kVA = Peak Load / Power Factor / 1000

tagline: Calculate string tension in pounds for any string gauge, scale length, and tuning

faq.question: What is ideal guitar string tension?
faq.answer: Most players prefer 14-18 lbs per string. Lower tension (10-14 lbs) is easier for bending. Higher tension (18-25 lbs) gives more volume and sustain but requires more finger strength.

faq.question: How does scale length affect tension?
faq.answer: Longer scale length increases tension at the same tuning. A Fender 25.5-inch scale has higher tension than a Gibson 24.75-inch scale with identical strings and tuning, which is why Gibson-scale guitars feel slinkier.

formulas: Tension (lbs) = (UW x (2 x Scale Length x Frequency)^2) / 386.4
Unit Weight = String Constant x (Gauge x 1000)^2

tagline: Calculate RT60 reverberation time for a room based on dimensions and surface materials

faq.question: What is RT60?
faq.answer: RT60 is the time it takes for sound to decay by 60 dB after the source stops. A recording studio aims for 0.2-0.4 seconds, a living room is typically 0.5-0.8 seconds, and a concert hall is 1.5-2.5 seconds.

faq.question: How can I reduce reverb time?
faq.answer: Add absorption: acoustic panels, thick curtains, carpet, upholstered furniture, and bookshelves all absorb sound. Covering 30-50% of wall area with 2-4 inch acoustic panels dramatically reduces RT60.

formulas: RT60 = 0.049 x Volume / Total Absorption (Sabine equation)
Total Absorption = Surface Area x Average Absorption Coefficient

tagline: Calculate the wattage needed for a PA system based on venue size and audience

faq.question: How many watts per person for a PA system?
faq.answer: Rule of thumb: 5 watts per person indoors, 10-15 watts outdoors. A 200-person indoor concert needs about 1000W. Speech-only events need about half that.

faq.question: Do I need subwoofers?
faq.answer: For music, yes. Subs handle frequencies below 100Hz that main speakers cannot reproduce efficiently. For speech-only events, subs are unnecessary. DJ/electronic music benefits most from powerful subs.

formulas: Base Wattage = Audience Size x 5 watts
Total Wattage = Base x Venue Multiplier x Music Type Multiplier

tagline: Estimate vinyl record pressing costs per unit for different quantities and formats

faq.question: What is the minimum order for vinyl pressing?
faq.answer: Most pressing plants have a minimum of 100-300 units. Some offer short runs of 50-100 at a higher per-unit cost. Standard minimum is usually 300 for the best pricing.

faq.question: How long does vinyl pressing take?
faq.answer: Typical turnaround is 12-16 weeks from approved test pressing. Rush orders (8-10 weeks) cost 20-30% more. Always factor in 2-4 weeks for test pressing approval.

formulas: Per Unit Cost = (Base + Color + Jacket) x Quantity Discount
Total Cost = (Per Unit x Quantity) + Setup Fee

tagline: Estimate streaming royalties from Spotify, Apple Music, and other platforms

faq.question: How much does Spotify pay per stream?
faq.answer: Spotify pays approximately $0.003-0.005 per stream, averaging about $0.004. The exact rate varies by country, listener subscription type (free vs premium), and total platform streams that month.

faq.question: Which streaming platform pays the most?
faq.answer: Tidal pays the highest per-stream rate ($0.008-0.013), followed by Apple Music ($0.007-0.01). However, Spotify has by far the most users, so total earnings often favor Spotify despite lower per-stream rates.

formulas: Gross Revenue = Monthly Streams x Per-Stream Rate
Net Revenue = Gross x (1 - Distributor %)

tagline: Calculate audio file sizes, bit rates, and frequency response for different sample rates and bit depths

faq.question: What sample rate should I record at?
faq.answer: 44.1 kHz is sufficient for most music (captures up to 22 kHz, beyond human hearing). Record at 48 kHz for video projects, or 96 kHz if you plan heavy pitch-shifting or time-stretching in post-production.

faq.question: Does higher bit depth mean better sound?
faq.answer: Higher bit depth means more dynamic range (quieter noise floor), not necessarily better sound. 16-bit gives 96 dB range (fine for playback). 24-bit gives 144 dB range — essential for recording to capture quiet details without noise.

formulas: File Size = Sample Rate x Bit Depth x Channels x Duration / 8
Max Frequency = Sample Rate / 2 (Nyquist theorem)

tagline: Calculate break-even ticket prices and revenue projections for concerts and live events

faq.question: What percentage of venue capacity should I plan for?
faq.answer: Plan for 70-80% capacity for first-time events. Established events can plan for 85-95%. Selling out at 100% is rare for most shows — always budget conservatively.

faq.question: What are typical concert production costs?
faq.answer: Small venue (100-300): $500-2,000 for sound and lights. Mid-size (300-1000): $2,000-8,000. Large venue (1000+): $10,000-50,000+. This includes sound engineer, lighting tech, equipment rental.

formulas: Break-Even Price = Total Costs / Expected Attendees
Profit = (Ticket Price x Attendees) - Total Costs

tagline: Estimate visa fees and processing costs for popular travel destinations

faq.question: Which countries can US citizens visit without a visa?
faq.answer: US passport holders can visit 186 countries visa-free or with visa-on-arrival, including all of Europe (Schengen), UK, Japan, South Korea, Australia (ETA), and most of South America. China, India, and Russia require advance visas.

faq.question: How far in advance should I apply for a visa?
faq.answer: Apply 2-3 months before travel. Some countries like China require applying no more than 6 months in advance. Schengen visas should be applied for 15-90 days before departure.

formulas: Total Cost = (Visa Fee + Processing Surcharge) x Number of Applicants
Processing Surcharge = Base Fee x Speed Multiplier

tagline: Compare total costs of hotel stays versus Airbnb rentals including hidden fees

faq.question: Is Airbnb really cheaper than hotels?
faq.answer: It depends on length of stay. Airbnb cleaning fees are fixed, so short stays (1-2 nights) often cost more than hotels. For 5+ nights, Airbnb is typically 20-40% cheaper, especially for groups who can split a whole-home rental.

faq.question: What hidden fees should I watch for?
faq.answer: Hotels: resort fees ($25-50/night), parking ($20-40/night), WiFi. Airbnb: cleaning fees ($50-200), service fees (14%), extra guest fees, and checkout chores. Always compare total cost, not just nightly rate.

formulas: Hotel Total = (Nightly Rate x Nights) x (1 + Tax Rate %)
Airbnb Total = (Nightly Rate x Nights) + Cleaning Fee + Service Fee

tagline: Estimate travel insurance costs based on trip value, duration, and traveler age

faq.question: Is travel insurance worth it?
faq.answer: Yes for trips over $3,000, international travel, adventure activities, or travelers over 60. The typical cost is 4-12% of your trip. It covers trip cancellation, medical emergencies abroad, lost luggage, and evacuations.

faq.question: What does Cancel For Any Reason (CFAR) cover?
faq.answer: CFAR reimburses 50-75% of your trip cost if you cancel for any reason, including just changing your mind. It costs 40-60% more than standard policies and must usually be purchased within 14-21 days of your first trip deposit.

formulas: Premium = Trip Cost x Base Rate x Age Multiplier x Duration Multiplier
Typical range: 4-12% of total trip cost

tagline: Calculate the true cost of currency exchange including hidden fees and markup

faq.question: Where is the cheapest place to exchange currency?
faq.answer: Best rates: Wise/Revolut (0.3-0.5% markup), bank ATMs abroad (1-3% + small fee), credit cards with no foreign transaction fee. Worst: airport kiosks (5-12% markup), hotel desks, tourist area exchange shops.

faq.question: What is the mid-market rate?
faq.answer: The mid-market rate (also called interbank rate) is the midpoint between buy and sell prices on the global currency market. It is the fairest exchange rate. Any difference between this and your offered rate is profit for the exchanger.

formulas: Hidden Markup % = (Mid-Market Rate - Offered Rate) / Mid-Market Rate x 100
Total Cost = Fair Value - Amount You Receive

tagline: Calculate the cash value of your credit card points and airline miles

faq.question: What is the best way to redeem points?
faq.answer: Transfer to airline partners for business/first class flights gives the highest value (2-5+ cents per point). Travel portal bookings give standard value (1.25-1.5 cpp). Cash back gives the worst value (0.5-1.0 cpp).

faq.question: How much are hotel points worth?
faq.answer: Hotel points are worth 0.4-0.8 cents each on average. Marriott: ~0.7 cpp, Hilton: ~0.5 cpp, Hyatt: ~1.5 cpp (best hotel program). Always compare the points cost to the cash rate of the same room.

formulas: Cash Value = Points x Cents Per Point / 100
Cents Per Point = Base Value x Redemption Multiplier

tagline: Check luggage weight against airline limits and calculate overweight baggage fees

faq.question: What is the standard checked bag weight limit?
faq.answer: Most airlines allow 50 lbs (23 kg) per checked bag for economy. Business/first class typically allows 70 lbs (32 kg). Budget carriers may limit to 40 lbs. Always check your specific airline.

faq.question: How much are overweight baggage fees?
faq.answer: Typically: 51-70 lbs costs $100, 71-100 lbs costs $200, and over 100 lbs is $400 or prohibited. It is often cheaper to check a second bag ($35-45) than to pay overweight fees on one bag.

formulas: Overweight = Bag Weight - Airline Limit
Total Cost = (Bags x Bag Fee) + Overweight Fee

tagline: Compare international roaming, calling apps, and local SIM costs for travel

faq.question: What is the cheapest way to use your phone abroad?
faq.answer: Buy a local SIM card ($5-20 for 5-10GB data + local calls) or use eSIM services like Airalo. WiFi calling with WhatsApp/FaceTime is free. Carrier roaming at $10/day adds up fast on longer trips.

faq.question: Should I get an international phone plan?
faq.answer: For short trips (3-5 days), your carrier daily pass ($5-10/day) is convenient. For 7+ days, a local SIM or eSIM saves significantly. T-Mobile includes free international data with most plans.

formulas: Total Cost = Daily Rate x Trip Duration
Roaming Cost = Base Rate + Overage Data Charges

tagline: Calculate effective response time, input lag, and frame visibility for gaming monitors

faq.question: What refresh rate do I need for gaming?
faq.answer: 60Hz is minimum, 144Hz is the sweet spot for competitive gaming, and 240Hz+ offers diminishing but real returns for esports. Most players notice the biggest difference going from 60 to 144Hz.

faq.question: What is the difference between response time and input lag?
faq.answer: Response time (GtG) is how fast pixels change color — affects motion blur/ghosting. Input lag is the delay between GPU output and image appearing on screen — affects how responsive the game feels to your inputs.

formulas: Frame Time = 1000 / Refresh Rate (ms)
Total Latency = System Latency + Input Lag + (Frame Time / 2)

tagline: Compare GPU performance scores and calculate FPS estimates for different resolutions

faq.question: How many FPS do I need for smooth gaming?
faq.answer: 60 FPS is the baseline for smooth gameplay. 144 FPS is ideal for competitive gaming with a high-refresh monitor. Beyond 240 FPS offers diminishing returns unless you are a professional esports player.

faq.question: Should I get NVIDIA or AMD?
faq.answer: NVIDIA excels in ray tracing and DLSS upscaling. AMD offers better price-to-performance at mid-range. For pure rasterization, they trade blows. NVIDIA is preferred for streaming (NVENC encoder) and AI workloads.

formulas: Estimated FPS = Base GPU FPS x Resolution Scale x Game Complexity Scale
1% Low = Average FPS x 0.65

tagline: Estimate monthly hosting costs for game servers based on player count and game type

faq.question: How much RAM does a game server need?
faq.answer: Minecraft: 1-2GB for 10 players, 4-8GB for 30+. FPS games: 2-4GB for most. Survival games (Rust, ARK): 8-16GB for 30+ players. More mods/plugins = more RAM needed.

faq.question: Should I use a managed host or VPS?
faq.answer: Managed hosts are easiest — one-click setup, auto-updates, game panel included. VPS is cheaper but requires Linux knowledge. Dedicated servers offer best performance for large communities (50+ regular players).

formulas: Monthly Cost = (RAM x $4 + Cores x $8) x Hosting Multiplier x Location Multiplier
RAM Needed = Players x RAM-per-Player Factor

tagline: Calculate prize pool distribution across teams and players for esports tournaments

faq.question: How are esports prize pools typically distributed?
faq.answer: First place typically gets 30-50% of the total pool. Top-heavy distributions (50%+ to winner) are common in smaller events. Major tournaments spread prizes more evenly to support the competitive ecosystem.

faq.question: What cut do esports organizations take?
faq.answer: Organizations typically take 10-30% of tournament winnings. Top organizations take less (10-15%) as they profit from sponsorships. Smaller orgs may take 20-30%. Some players negotiate better splits based on performance.

formulas: Placement Prize = Total Pool x (Placement Share / Sum of All Shares)
Player Earnings = (Team Prize x (1 - Org Cut)) / Team Size

tagline: Find ideal gaming chair and desk settings based on your height and body measurements

faq.question: What is the correct gaming chair height?
faq.answer: Your feet should be flat on the floor with thighs parallel to the ground. Knees should be at approximately 90 degrees. Most people need a seat height of 16-21 inches depending on leg length.

faq.question: How far should a gaming monitor be?
faq.answer: The general rule is 1.2x the monitor diagonal. A 27-inch monitor should be about 32 inches away. For ultrawide monitors, increase distance by 20%. Your eyes should align with the top third of the screen.

formulas: Seat Height = Leg Length + 1 inch
Monitor Distance = Monitor Size (diagonal) x 1.2

tagline: Calculate optimal streaming bitrate for Twitch, YouTube, and other platforms

faq.question: What bitrate should I stream at on Twitch?
faq.answer: Twitch caps non-partners at 6000 kbps. For 1080p60, use 6000 kbps. For 720p60, use 3500-4500 kbps. If you lack Transcoding options (non-partner), consider 720p so viewers with slow connections can watch.

faq.question: How much upload speed do I need to stream?
faq.answer: Your upload should be at least 1.5x your bitrate. For 6000 kbps streaming, you need at least 9 Mbps upload. 20+ Mbps is ideal to leave headroom for the game connection and other traffic.

formulas: Upload Usage % = Stream Bitrate / (Upload Speed x 1000) x 100
Recommended: Upload Speed > 1.5x Stream Bitrate

tagline: Calculate water savings from upgrading to a low-flow or dual-flush toilet

faq.question: How much water does an old toilet use?
faq.answer: Toilets made before 1994 use 3.5-7 gallons per flush. Models from 1994-2004 use 1.6 GPF. Current WaterSense certified toilets use 1.28 GPF or less. Dual-flush models use 0.8 GPF for liquid waste.

faq.question: Are low-flow toilets effective?
faq.answer: Modern 1.28 GPF toilets perform as well or better than old 3.5 GPF models thanks to improved engineering. Pressure-assist and gravity-fed designs ensure effective flushing with less water.

formulas: Daily Savings = (Old GPF - New GPF) x Flushes Per Day
Annual Cost Savings = (Annual Gallons / 1000) x Water Rate

tagline: Size a water softener based on household size, water hardness, and daily usage

faq.question: How do I know my water hardness?
faq.answer: Get a free test from your water utility or buy a test kit ($10-15). Hardness is measured in grains per gallon (GPG). 0-3 GPG is soft, 3-7 is moderate, 7-15 is hard, and 15+ is very hard.

faq.question: How often does a water softener regenerate?
faq.answer: Most softeners regenerate every 3-7 days depending on usage and hardness. A properly sized unit for a family of 4 with 15 GPG water regenerates about once per week using 6-8 lbs of salt.

formulas: Daily Grains = People x Daily Gallons x Effective Hardness
Effective Hardness = Water Hardness + (Iron ppm x 5)

tagline: Calculate the minimum septic tank size based on bedrooms and daily water usage

faq.question: How big of a septic tank do I need?
faq.answer: Most codes require: 1-2 bedrooms = 750 gallons, 3 bedrooms = 1000 gallons, 4 bedrooms = 1250 gallons. Always size up if you have a garbage disposal, hot tub, or high-efficiency washer.

faq.question: How often should a septic tank be pumped?
faq.answer: Every 3-5 years for a typical family of 4 with a 1000-gallon tank. Smaller tanks or larger families need more frequent pumping. Have the tank inspected every 1-2 years.

formulas: Minimum Tank = Base Size (by bedrooms) x Disposal Multiplier + Laundry Adder
Drain Field = Daily Flow x 1.5 sq ft

tagline: Calculate pipe insulation material needed and energy savings for hot or cold water pipes

faq.question: Which pipes should be insulated?
faq.answer: Insulate hot water pipes to reduce heat loss and save energy (2-4% on water heating). Insulate cold water pipes in unheated areas to prevent freezing. Both hot and cold pipes in crawl spaces and attics should be insulated.

faq.question: What type of pipe insulation is best?
faq.answer: Foam tubes are cheapest and easiest to install (DIY-friendly). Fiberglass offers higher R-value for extreme temperatures. Rubber (Armaflex) is best for condensation prevention on cold pipes and commercial applications.

formulas: Material Needed = Pipe Length x 1.10 (waste factor)
Annual Savings = Length x Temp Diff x Rate x Heat Reduction %

tagline: Calculate the right sump pump size based on basement area, water table, and discharge height

faq.question: What size sump pump do I need?
faq.answer: For most homes: 1/3 HP handles up to 1800 GPH, sufficient for average basements. 1/2 HP (3000 GPH) for larger basements or moderate water. 3/4-1 HP for high water table areas or long discharge runs.

faq.question: Do I need a backup sump pump?
faq.answer: Yes if your area floods frequently or you have valuable items in the basement. Battery backup pumps run 5-7 hours during power outages. A water alarm ($15-25) is the minimum every homeowner should have.

formulas: Total Head = Vertical Lift + (Horizontal Run x 0.1)
Required GPH = Basement Area x 1.5 x Water Risk Multiplier x Head Loss Factor

tagline: Size a tankless water heater based on flow rate needs and temperature rise

faq.question: What size tankless water heater do I need?
faq.answer: Calculate total flow rate (showers 2 GPM, faucets 1.5 GPM, dishwasher 1.5 GPM) and temperature rise (desired temp minus incoming water temp). A family of 4 typically needs 150,000-199,000 BTU gas or 24-36 kW electric.

faq.question: Is a tankless water heater worth it?
faq.answer: Tankless heaters save 20-30% on water heating costs and last 20+ years (vs 10-15 for tank). Higher upfront cost ($1,000-3,000 installed) is offset by energy savings. Best for homes using under 86 gallons/day.

formulas: Required BTU = Flow Rate (GPM) x Temperature Rise x 500
Electric kW = BTU / 3412

tagline: Calculate decking boards, screws, and joist material needed for your deck project

faq.question: How much waste factor should I add for decking?
faq.answer: Add 10% for a standard rectangular deck. Add 15-20% for angled cuts, picture-frame borders, or complex shapes. Composite boards are more expensive, so measure carefully to minimize waste.

faq.question: What is the best decking material?
faq.answer: Pressure treated is cheapest and lasts 15-20 years with maintenance. Cedar looks great and lasts 15-25 years. Composite costs more upfront but lasts 25-50 years with zero maintenance. Ipe is the most durable (75+ years) but very expensive.

formulas: Boards Needed = (Deck Width / Board Width) x 1.10 waste factor
Total Linear Feet = Boards x Deck Length

tagline: Calculate concrete volume in cubic yards, bags, and cost for slabs, footings, and pads

faq.question: How thick should a concrete slab be?
faq.answer: 4 inches for patios and walkways. 6 inches for driveways and garages. 8+ inches for heavy equipment pads. Always pour on 4 inches of compacted gravel base with proper drainage.

faq.question: Should I use ready-mix or bags?
faq.answer: Use bags for under 1 cubic yard (small projects). Over 1 cubic yard, ready-mix is far cheaper and easier. A typical truck minimum is 1 yard. For 2+ cubic yards, ready-mix saves significant time and labor.

formulas: Cubic Yards = (Length x Width x Thickness/12) / 27
80-lb Bags = Cubic Feet / 0.6

tagline: Calculate rain gutter and downspout sizing based on roof area and rainfall intensity

faq.question: What size gutters do I need?
faq.answer: 5-inch K-style gutters handle most residential homes (up to 5,500 sq ft of roof area per gutter run). 6-inch gutters are needed for large roofs, steep pitches, or heavy rainfall areas (4+ inches/hour).

faq.question: How many downspouts do I need?
faq.answer: One downspout for every 20-40 feet of gutter run. A 40-foot run needs at least one, but two (at each end) is better for drainage. Each 2x3 inch downspout handles about 600 sq ft of roof area.

formulas: Drainage Area = Gutter Run x Roof Width x Pitch Factor
Flow Rate (GPM) = Drainage Area x Rainfall / 96.23

tagline: Estimate window replacement costs including materials, labor, and energy savings

faq.question: How much do replacement windows cost?
faq.answer: Average cost is $400-700 per window for double-pane vinyl (installed). Budget $300-400 for single pane, $600-1,100 for triple pane, and $700-1,500 for impact-resistant. A whole-house project (10-15 windows) typically runs $5,000-15,000.

faq.question: Are energy-efficient windows worth the cost?
faq.answer: Double-pane Low-E windows can reduce energy bills by 10-15%. Triple-pane saves 15-25%. The payback period is typically 10-20 years from energy savings alone, but they also improve comfort and resale value.

formulas: Per Window = Material Cost x Frame Multiplier + Labor
Payback Period = Total Cost / Annual Energy Savings

tagline: Calculate the correct rough opening framing dimensions for interior and exterior doors

faq.question: What is a rough opening?
faq.answer: The rough opening is the framed opening in the wall that receives the door (or window). It is always larger than the door itself to allow for the door frame, shims, and adjustment. Typically 2 inches wider and 2.5 inches taller than the door.

faq.question: What size header do I need above a door?
faq.answer: For interior non-load-bearing walls: a flat 2x4 is sufficient. For load-bearing walls: doubled 2x6 up to 4 ft, 2x8 up to 6 ft, 2x10 up to 8 ft opening. Always check local code for load-bearing walls.

formulas: Rough Opening Width = Door Width + 2 inches (pre-hung)
Rough Opening Height = Door Height + 2.5 inches (pre-hung)

tagline: Calculate lumber, siding, roofing, and hardware needed to build a backyard shed

faq.question: How much does it cost to build a shed?
faq.answer: DIY material cost: $1,500-4,000 for an 8x10 to 12x16 shed. Pre-built kits run $2,000-6,000. Hiring a contractor doubles the cost. The biggest expenses are plywood/siding (30%), roofing (20%), and lumber (25%).

faq.question: Do I need a permit to build a shed?
faq.answer: Most areas require a permit for sheds over 120-200 sq ft or over 10 feet tall. Sheds under 100 sq ft often do not require permits. Always check local building codes — setback requirements, maximum height, and lot coverage limits apply.

formulas: Studs = Perimeter / 1.33 (16" O.C.) + 8 (corners/headers)
Plywood Sheets = (Wall Area + Roof Area + Floor Area) / 32 sq ft per sheet

tagline: Calculate baker's percentages and ingredient weights for pizza dough at any hydration level

faq.question: What hydration should pizza dough be?
faq.answer: Neapolitan: 60-65%. New York style: 63-67%. Thin and crispy: 55-60%. Sicilian/pan: 70-80%. Higher hydration gives a lighter, more open crumb but is harder to handle. Start at 63% and adjust.

faq.question: What is baker's percentage?
faq.answer: Baker's percentage expresses each ingredient as a percentage of the flour weight. Flour is always 100%. So 65% hydration means 650g water for every 1000g flour. This makes scaling recipes easy.

formulas: Flour = Total Dough x (100 / (100 + Hydration% + Salt% + Yeast%))
Water = Flour x Hydration%

tagline: Calculate dilution rates, final ABV, and water content for shaken and stirred cocktails

faq.question: How much does ice dilute a cocktail?
faq.answer: Stirring adds 15-20% dilution (about 0.5-0.75 oz per drink). Shaking adds 25-30% (about 1 oz). The dilution is intentional — it opens up flavors and integrates ingredients. Dave Arnold measured this extensively.

faq.question: What ABV should a finished cocktail be?
faq.answer: Classic cocktails target 18-25% ABV. A Martini is about 28-32% (very spirit-forward). A Margarita is about 15-18%. Long drinks (Highball, G&T) are 8-12%. Below 10% and the drink can taste watery; above 30% it may seem harsh.

formulas: Water Added = Pre-Dilution Volume x Dilution Rate
Final ABV = (Spirit oz x Spirit ABV%) / Total Volume

tagline: Estimate annual energy output and savings from a small residential wind turbine

faq.question: Are small wind turbines worth it?
faq.answer: Small wind is viable in rural areas with average winds above 10 mph and electricity rates above $0.12/kWh. Payback is typically 10-20 years. They work best combined with solar since wind and sun often complement each other seasonally.

faq.question: How much wind do I need for a turbine?
faq.answer: Minimum 9 mph average annual wind speed for economic viability. 12+ mph is ideal. Wind power scales with the cube of wind speed, so a site with 14 mph wind produces nearly 3x more power than one with 10 mph.

formulas: Power (W) = 0.5 x Air Density x Rotor Area x Wind Speed^3 x Efficiency
Annual kWh = Rated kW x 8760 hours x Capacity Factor

tagline: Calculate how long a battery backup system will power your essential loads

faq.question: How long will a Tesla Powerwall last in an outage?
faq.answer: A single Powerwall (13.5 kWh) lasts 10-12 hours powering essential loads (fridge, lights, WiFi, phones = ~1.2 kW), or 4-5 hours with heavier loads including AC. Two Powerwalls can typically get through a full day.

faq.question: What should I power during an outage?
faq.answer: Priority loads: refrigerator (0.15 kW), lights (0.1 kW), WiFi router (0.02 kW), phone charging (0.01 kW). Total essentials ~0.5 kW. Adding a well pump (1 kW), sump pump (0.5 kW), or AC (3 kW) drastically reduces runtime.

formulas: Usable Energy = Battery kWh x Usable % x Inverter Efficiency %
Runtime (hours) = Usable Energy / Load (kW)

tagline: Estimate OSHA penalty amounts based on violation type, severity, and company size

faq.question: What are 2024 OSHA maximum fines?
faq.answer: Serious/Other: up to $16,131 per violation. Willful/Repeat: up to $161,323 per violation. Failure to Abate: $16,131 per day. Penalties are adjusted annually for inflation. Multiple violations at one worksite can result in millions in total fines.

faq.question: How can OSHA penalties be reduced?
faq.answer: Reductions for: small business size (up to 60% for under 25 employees), good faith/safety programs (up to 25%), and compliance history (up to 10%). Contesting citations through an informal conference can also reduce penalties.

formulas: Base Penalty = Maximum for Violation Type x Severity Multiplier
Final Penalty = Base x (1 - Size Reduction - Good Faith Reduction)

tagline: Estimate costs for ADA accessibility retrofits for commercial buildings

faq.question: Is ADA compliance required for my business?
faq.answer: Yes if your business is open to the public (Title III) or has 15+ employees (Title I). Existing buildings must remove barriers when readily achievable. New construction and major renovations must be fully ADA compliant.

faq.question: What are common ADA violations?
faq.answer: Most common: no accessible entrance (ramp), non-compliant restrooms (grab bars, clearance), doorways under 32 inches clear, no accessible parking, missing Braille/tactile signage, and inaccessible counters/service areas.

formulas: Total Cost = Ramp + Restroom + Doorways + Signage
Doorway Cost = Number of Doorways x $1,500 average

tagline: Calculate return on investment for common home renovation projects at resale

faq.question: What home renovation has the best ROI?
faq.answer: Garage door replacement leads with 95%+ ROI. Exterior improvements (siding, roofing) recoup 60-80%. Kitchen and bath mid-range remodels return 70-80%. High-end renovations return less percentage-wise but add more absolute value.

faq.question: Should I renovate before selling?
faq.answer: Focus on curb appeal (paint, landscaping, garage door) and essential repairs (roof, HVAC). Minor kitchen updates (paint cabinets, new hardware, countertops) have high ROI. Avoid major renovations unless the home needs them to sell.

formulas: Value Added = Project Cost x ROI Rate
Net Cost = Project Cost - Value Added

tagline: Estimate potential savings from appealing your property tax assessment

faq.question: How do I know if my property is over-assessed?
faq.answer: Compare your assessed value to recent sales of similar homes (same size, age, condition, neighborhood). If your assessment is 10%+ higher than comparable sales, you likely have grounds for an appeal. Check your county assessor website for comps.

faq.question: What is the success rate for property tax appeals?
faq.answer: About 50-70% of formal appeals result in some reduction. Having 3-5 comparable sales supporting a lower value significantly increases your chances. Many homeowners never appeal, even when over-assessed.

formulas: Annual Savings = (Assessed Value - Fair Market Value) x Tax Rate
5-Year Savings = (Annual Savings x 5) - Filing Fee
